region_of_origin: Singapore (Chinese dessert tradition; taro sourced from Guangxi, China; durian from Thailand)
food_subgroup: Chilled milk-based layered dessert (tang shui / Chinese sweet soup dessert style)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for durian-taro double-layer milk custard. FOODON_03315091 (custard food product) is the closest parent category covering milk-based set desserts.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW (Milk-based desserts) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for durian-taro layered milk custard in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to this specific dessert preparation. SNOMED CT does not cover specialty Asian dessert items at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately one cup or bowl of approximately 200 g, as typically served at a Chinese dessert shop (tang shui pu) in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component ingredients: USDA FoodData Central — Durian raw (FDC ID 168162), Taro cooked (FDC ID 170071), Whole milk (FDC ID 171265), Sugar (FDC ID 169655); proportions estimated at approximately 25% durian, 30% taro puree, 35% whole milk, 10% sugar by weight
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact match for durian-taro double-layer milk custard exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component blending: durian raw ~25 g per 100 g final product, cooked taro puree ~30 g, whole milk ~35 g, sugar ~10 g. This is a medium-confidence estimate. Values cross-checked for plausibility against similar Southeast Asian milk-based desserts.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of taro (Colocasia esculenta) and analogous milk-based dessert estimation; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283; taro GI ~53-56, whole milk GI ~27-34, durian GI ~49-50 (estimated from tropical fruit data)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for durian-taro double-layer milk custard. GI estimated as weighted blend: taro cooked GI ~54 (weight 30%), whole milk GI ~31 (weight 35%), durian GI ~50 (weight 25%), sugar GI ~65 (weight 10%). Weighted composite GI ≈ 52. GL per 200 g serving = (52 × 24.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 25.5. Classified as low GI (≤55). Source: University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care international GI tables.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~28 mg per 100 g); potassium-rich from taro and durian (~210 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: With approximately 56 mg sodium per 200 g serving and a favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio contributed by taro and durian, this dessert poses low hypertension risk when consumed occasionally, though total caloric load should be monitored in individuals managing weight-related blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~25.5 per serving) and significant sugar content (~14 g per 100 g) from added sugar and naturally occurring fruit sugars in durian
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to half a serving (approximately 100 g) given the moderate glycemic load of 25.5 per standard serving and the combined contribution of added sugar and durian's naturally high fructose and glucose content; pairing with a protein-rich food may help blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content (~2.4 g per 100 g) from whole milk and durian; moderate energy density (~148 kcal per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat contribution from whole milk and durian flesh (~4.8 g per 200 g serving) is moderate; individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional dessert and consider requesting reduced-sugar or reduced-milk-fat preparation where available.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Durian and Taro Double-Layer Milk Custard is a chilled Chinese-style layered dessert (tang shui style) served at specialty Chinese dessert shops (tang shui pu) in Singapore. It consists of two distinct layers: a lower layer of dense, handcrafted taro puree made from premium Guangxi Lipu taro, prized for its floury texture and intense purple-flecked aroma, and an upper layer of rich, creamy milk custard blended with Thai Golden Pillow durian flesh, known for its bittersweet, complex flavour and custardy consistency. The dessert is typically set with gelatin or agar-agar and served chilled. The flavour profile combines the earthy, starchy sweetness of taro with the pungent, tropical richness of durian, unified by the mild creaminess of fresh whole milk. Nutritionally, each 200 g serving provides approximately 296 kcal, 5.6 g protein, 9.0 g fat, and 49 g carbohydrate, with a moderate glycemic load of approximately 25.5. The dessert contains meaningful potassium (~420 mg per serving) and calcium (~170 mg per serving) from taro and milk. Clinically, the moderate sugar content and glycemic load require portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, while the saturated fat from durian and whole milk warrants moderation for those managing cardiovascular risk. The dish is gluten-free and halal-suitable, making it accessible to a broad population in Singapore's multicultural food landscape.

confidence_reason: No exact match for this specific dish exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component blending of USDA FoodData Central entries for durian (raw), cooked taro, whole milk, and sugar. GI is an analogous estimate derived from University of Sydney GI Database values for component ingredients. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-blend estimates based on USDA FoodData Central data for durian raw (FDC 168162), cooked taro (FDC 170071), whole milk (FDC 171265), and sugar (FDC 169655) at approximate proportions of 25:30:35:10 by weight. No direct laboratory analysis or approved-source entry exists for this specific dish. 2. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this dessert contains no hydrogenated fats; all ingredients are natural whole foods or minimally processed dairy. 4. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Beverages' to 'Desserts', which accurately reflects the nature of this chilled layered custard dessert. 5. All numeric fields in per_100g are populated from component estimates; no sentinel -1 values are present. 6. Halal status is assumed based on typical ingredients; gelatin source (bovine vs. porcine vs. plant-based agar) should be confirmed with the restaurant for strict halal compliance. 7. Price data (SGD 11.60) preserved from input source (field survey data) for reference only; not used as a nutritional evidence source.

improvement_actions_taken: Added mandatory Thai local name and aliases to `local_names.thai`, `aliases_by_language.thai`, and the flat `aliases` list to address G6, Q1, and Q8 failures from the previous iteration. This was done to comply with validator feedback, which indicated Thai was a mandatory language despite the primary cuisine being Chinese/Singaporean, likely due to the 'durian from Thailand' mention in the region of origin.
